output_summary: aligned schemas for datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small; generated 2 schema correspondence groups.
profiled datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small.
Reused the blocking configuration from state.
Blocking uses `semantic_similarity` for goodreads_small_amazon_small, metabooks_small_amazon_small, metabooks_small_goodreads_small, blocking on title, author, publish_year.
Performance-wise, pair completeness ranges from 0.99 to 1.00 and candidate counts range from 149824 to 199823.
The config also records dataset id columns.

matcher mode `rule_based` with config source reused from state; evaluated pairs: id_columns, matching_strategies; selected scores: matching_strategies (threshold=0.66, f1=0.8029330889092575, threshold=0.72, f1=0.852589641434263, threshold=0.68); produced 2 pair strategy entries.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline.

The pipeline loaded three parquet datasets, aligned schemas by renaming Goodreads and Metabooks columns to the Amazon schema using an LLM-based schema matcher, and then performed pairwise entity matching across all three source pairs.
The matching stage used embedding-based blocking for Goodreads–Amazon, Metabooks–Amazon, and Metabooks–Goodreads with specified text columns and top_k settings before applying a rule-based matcher.
The rule-based matcher used pair-specific comparator sets, weights, and thresholds for the three dataset pairs and wrote each correspondence table to output files.

The fusion stage concatenated all pairwise correspondences and introduced attribute-level fusers for 12 fields, using longest_string for text-heavy fields, maximum for numeric fields, and union for genres.
The run established a pair-specific matching strategy instead of a single uniform matcher configuration by varying blocker text fields, comparator choices, weights, and thresholds by dataset pair.
Fusion behavior shifted to a rule-based attribute-wise resolution strategy that prefers longer textual values, larger numeric values, and merged genre sets across matched records.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.

cluster_analysis flagged output/correspondences/correspondences_metabooks_small_goodreads_small.csv as unhealthy; diagnosis: many entities have multiple close-scoring matches; recommended_action=update_matching_config; matching_config artifact changed/updated; health metrics total_clusters=1073,max_cluster_size=8,small_cluster_ratio=0.9441,large_cluster_ratio=0.0075,is_long_tail=true,one_to_many_ratio=0.0476,max_degree=6,ambiguous_ratio=0.7222; next node remains PENDING.
This run was an initial draft of the adapted pipeline.
The pipeline changed blocking preparation so that publish_year is no longer cast in place and instead a new helper column named publish_year_blocking is created as a string for blocking use only.
The goodreads_small to amazon_small EmbeddingBlocker was updated to use text columns title, author, and publish_year_blocking instead of title, author, and publish_year.
The rule-based matcher weights were retuned across all three dataset pairs while keeping the comparator sets and thresholds unchanged.
Matching behavior shifted toward stronger reliance on title similarity across all pairwise matchers by increasing the title weight in each weight vector.

Matching behavior shifted away from author and publish_year contributions, with lower weights for those signals in every pairwise matcher, while publisher and genres received relatively more emphasis in some pairs.
Fusion behavior did not shift, because the attribute fusers and singleton handling remained unchanged.

The original numeric publish_year field is preserved for NumericComparator matching and maximum-based fusion, while the new publish_year_blocking helper is used only to satisfy EmbeddingBlocker text input requirements.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 2; fusion_data.csv was produced.

pipeline.py changed blocking prep to preserve numeric publish_year for matching/fusion by adding publish_year_blocking = df["publish_year"].astype("string") instead of overwriting df["publish_year"], and switched EmbeddingBlocker goodreads_small↔amazon_small text_cols from ["title","author","publish_year"] to ["title","author","publish_year_blocking"]; matcher weights changed to [0.52,0.26,0.08,0.14], [0.58,0.19,0.05,0.18], and [0.5,0.16,0.06,0.18,0.1].
This run was an initial draft adaptation of the pipeline.
The matching weights were changed for the goodreads_small versus amazon_small matcher from [0.52, 0.26, 0.08, 0.14] to [0.64, 0.17, 0.04, 0.15].
The matching weights were changed for the metabooks_small versus amazon_small matcher from [0.58, 0.19, 0.05, 0.18] to [0.62, 0.14, 0.04, 0.2].
The pipeline now creates the output/data_fusion directory before writing fusion_data.csv.
The matching behavior shifted toward stronger reliance on title similarity in the goodreads_small versus amazon_small and metabooks_small versus amazon_small matchers.
The matching behavior shifted away from author and publish_year evidence in those two matchers, while publisher gained a small increase in influence for both.
The metabooks_small versus goodreads_small matcher, the blocking setup, the thresholds, and the fusion strategy remained unchanged.
The comment describing matching configuration was updated to say the weights came from latest cluster-analysis feedback.
The helper publish_year_blocking string column preparation remained in place for embedding blocking while numeric publish_year stayed available for matching and fusion.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 3; fusion_data.csv was produced.

matching_config artifact changed in pipeline.py: comment revised to "Updated weights from latest cluster-analysis feedback"; RuleBasedMatcher weights for goodreads_small↔amazon_small changed [0.52, 0.26, 0.08, 0.14]→[0.64, 0.17, 0.04, 0.15]; weights for metabooks_small↔amazon_small changed [0.58, 0.19, 0.05, 0.18]→[0.62, 0.14, 0.04, 0.2]; added os.makedirs("output/data_fusion", exist_ok=True); cluster summary/result fields were written for this run.
This run produced an initial draft of the evaluation script for the fused books output.
The dominant strategy was to build an evaluation-aligned fused view by mapping fused clusters to gold IDs through parsed _fusion_sources and then evaluate fields with schema-specific match functions.
Textual singleton fields title, author, language, and publisher were grouped under tokenized_match evaluation.
Numeric fields publish_year and page_count were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match evaluation.
The genres field and any additional detected list-like non-system columns were grouped under set_equality_match evaluation after list normalization.
The script added _parse_source_ids to robustly parse _fusion_sources values from lists or serialized containers using json.loads and ast.literal_eval.
The script added _detect_gold_prefix to infer the dominant gold ID prefix and improve source-to-gold candidate selection.
The script added build_eval_view to expand fused rows by candidate gold-linked source IDs, attach eval_id and _eval_cluster_id, sort by _fusion_confidence, and deduplicate on eval_id.
The script added a fallback path that uses _id as eval_id when no expanded evaluation rows can be built.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 55.00%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
The run is failing mainly because noisy matched clusters are being fused with naive attribute rules, which produces incorrect fused values from wrong source records.
The run also has severe representation and normalization errors in fusion outputs, especially for genres, authors, and publishers.
The run additionally has coverage gaps where some gold entities are missing from fused output or evaluation alignment, likely because singleton clusters are excluded.
Overall accuracy is 0.55 with 77 correct evaluations out of 140 across 20 records and 7 attributes.
Genres accuracy is 0.0, which is the clearest signal of a structural fusion or normalization failure rather than a minor formatting issue.
Author accuracy is 0.35, while publisher accuracy is 0.6, title accuracy is 0.6, page_count accuracy is 0.65, publish_year accuracy is 0.75, and language accuracy is 0.9.
The analysis cites clearly wrong fused values such as expected author Scott Cunningham but fused author John C. Sulak and Oberon Zell-Ravenheart.
The analysis also cites expected author Leonard B. Scott but fused author Graham Masterton, expected page_count 240 but fused 448, and expected publisher Minotaur Books but fused Thomas Dunne Books.
This run was an evaluation-driven refinement rather than an initial draft or a repair.
There were no material pipeline logic changes in this run because the before and after pipelines kept the same blocking, matching, thresholds, fusion rules, and singleton setting.
The after pipeline only made minor comment and code-order cleanups, such as clarifying helper-column comments and moving output directory creation before engine construction.

The fusion configuration remained unchanged with longest_string for title, author, publisher, language, bookformat, and edition, maximum for publish_year, rating, numratings, page_count, and price, and union for genres.
There was no implemented strategy shift in fusion or matching behavior in this run.

The evaluation analysis recommended a future shift toward fusion that is more robust to noisy clusters by preferring consensus and normalization over longest_string, maximum, and raw union, but that shift was not applied here.

The analysis also recommended defending against overmerged Goodreads–Amazon clusters with source-aware and agreement-based fusion, but the live pipeline still fused all matched records using the prior rule set.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.

matching_config artifact changed; pipeline.py comments now state EmbeddingBlocker text_cols must exist in both datasets, use supplied matching config/latest cluster-analysis weights, add preprocess mapping note `lower_strip -> lambda x: str(x).lower().strip()`, and move `os.makedirs("output/data_fusion", exist_ok=True)` earlier.
This run was a refinement of the evaluation setup rather than an initial draft or a repair.

The dominant strategy kept a field-specific evaluation scheme that aligned tokenized matching to text fields, numeric tolerance matching to numeric fields, and set equality matching to list-like fields while preserving an evaluation-aligned fused view built from fusion source IDs.
Title, author, language, and publisher were grouped under tokenized_match evaluation.
Publish_year and page_count were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match evaluation.
Genres and any detected list-like non-system columns were grouped under set_equality_match evaluation.
The run retained helper logic that parses _fusion_sources, detects the dominant gold ID prefix, and expands fused rows into eval_id-aligned records before deduplication.
The run preserved list-like column detection and normalization across fused and gold data to stabilize set-based comparisons.
The fallback import path handling for list_normalization was simplified by removing the conditional Path.cwd() alternatives tied to __file__ checks and using direct parent path resolutions.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 55.00%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
The run is primarily failing in data fusion because wrongly merged clusters and naive fusers are selecting incorrect canonical values.
The run also has a fusion coverage problem because unmatched entities are omitted when include_singletons is set to false.
The run has substantial normalization and representation issues, especially for authors, titles, publishers, and genres.
Overall accuracy is 0.55 with 77 correct evaluations out of 140.
Genres accuracy is 0.0, which is the worst attribute-specific failure in the run.
Author accuracy is 0.35, while title accuracy is 0.6, publisher accuracy is 0.6, publish_year accuracy is 0.75, language accuracy is 0.9, and page_count accuracy is 0.65.
Blocking pair completeness is reported as very high at approximately 0.994 to 0.998, so missed blocking is unlikely to be the dominant failure mode.
Debug mismatches show fused values coming from different books entirely, including metabooks_206748, metabooks_112407, metabooks_136165, and metabooks_410403.
Several records show fused_id equal to NaN or fused_value equal to null, including metabooks_301818 and amazon_12422.
This run was an evaluation-driven refinement attempt rather than an initial draft or a repair with implemented code changes.
No concrete pipeline changes were actually applied in this run because the before and after pipelines remained identical.
The fusion configuration still uses longest_string for title, author, publisher, language, bookformat, and edition.
The fusion configuration still uses maximum for publish_year, rating, numratings, page_count, and price, and still uses union for genres.
The pipeline still runs DataFusionEngine with include_singletons set to False.
The evaluation analysis recommended shifting emphasis away from blocking changes and toward making fusion more robust to residual false positive matches.
The recommended fusion behavior was to move from verbosity-based and extremum-based selection toward normalized consensus-based, source-aware, and conflict-resilient attribute fusion.
The analysis also recommended treating genres as atomic normalized labels before union rather than preserving coarse list or comma-joined representations.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.

matching_config artifact changed from cluster-analysis feedback; pipeline.py comments now state EmbeddingBlocker needs shared text columns and publish_year_blocking is a string helper for blocking, and matching config uses latest cluster-analysis updates.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or a repair.
The changes refined the evaluation script structure, diagnostics, and reporting while keeping the same core attribute evaluators.

The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation around source-ID-aligned fused-to-gold mapping, list-like normalization, and attribute-specific comparison functions that better match fused output behavior.

The run kept tokenized matching for title, author, language, and publisher, numeric tolerance matching for publish_year and page_count, and set equality matching for genres and other detected list-like fields.
Textual singleton fields were grouped under tokenized_match for title, author, language, and publisher.
Numeric fields were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match for publish_year and page_count.
Collection-like fields were grouped under set_equality_match for genres and any additional detected list-like columns present in both fused and gold data.
The script retained and used build_eval_view to align fused rows to gold IDs through parsed _fusion_sources and eval_id expansion.
